description: Where residential mortgage lending is material, the Board should take reasonable steps to satisfy itself as to the level of risk and effectiveness of the risk management framework, including addressing it in the risk appetite, risk management strategy and business plans.

The Board should seek assurances from senior management that the approved risk appetite is communicated to relevant persons and appropriately reflected in policies and procedures, and that a robust management information system is in place.

Where residential mortgage lending is material, the risk appetite statement should specifically address the residential mortgage portfolio and quantifiable risk limits should be set for various aspects of the portfolio.

A prudent ADI should set risk limits covering loan types with differing risk profiles, origination channels, geographic concentrations, serviceability criteria, LVR limits, LMI use, special circumstance loans, override frequency, default/arrears rates and non-lending losses.

The risk management framework should clearly specify whether risk limits are hard or soft limits, and APRA would be concerned if risk limits were frequently redefined in a manner that obscures limit breaches or leads to greater risk-taking.

Senior management should monitor compliance with material policies, procedures and risk limits and report material breaches or overrides to the Board; where limits are routinely breached, the Board and senior management should consider whether this reflects a less prudent lending culture.

Portfolios with higher inherent risk should be accompanied by stronger controls including increased senior management oversight, more granular reporting, increased risk management and internal audit reviews, stronger default management and provisioning and capital levels reflective of portfolio risk.

The risk management framework applicable to the residential mortgage lending portfolio should be subject to a comprehensive review by an operationally independent and competent person at least every three years, with results reported to the Board.
A prudent ADI should conduct a periodic assessment of compliance with responsible lending conduct obligations to ensure it does not expose itself to significant financial loss.

A prudent ADI with material exposure to residential mortgage lending should invest in management information systems that capture risk metrics at the point of application and throughout the life of the loan, and provide analytical capability to monitor and report key metrics against risk appetite.

A robust management information system should provide good quality information covering portfolio composition, performance reporting, compliance against risk limits, broker performance, override exception reporting, serviceability buffers, arrears, valuation changes, LMI tracking, provisioning trends and audit findings.

Where the residential mortgage lending portfolio is material, a prudent ADI should apply its remuneration policy to persons involved in residential mortgage lending, including remuneration of third parties such as mortgage broker firms responsible for originating a material proportion of the portfolio.

A prudent ADI should have appropriate measures to ensure commission-based broker compensation does not create adverse incentives, including consideration of clawback provisions and monitoring and controls to guard against incentives to pursue loans with inadequate verification, marginal serviceability or excessive leverage.

A prudent ADI relying on brokers to originate a material proportion of its portfolio should closely monitor performance at the broker firm or individual broker level, and take measures including restricting or terminating relationships where elevated defaults or materially deficient documentation are identified.

A prudent ADI should recognise and address the risks arising from different origination channels in its risk management framework, exercising higher diligence when credit approval decisions are made distant from the borrower or collateral location.

APRA expects an ADI to undertake a new serviceability assessment whenever there are material changes to the current or originally approved loan conditions, including changes in repayment basis, interest rate basis, loan tenor or any change that increases total repayments over the life of the loan.

Material serviceability policies and procedures should be reviewed at least annually and updated to align with the changing external environment; APRA expects material changes to serviceability policy to be analysed and their potential impact on the risk profile of new loans reported to appropriate risk governance forums.

Loan serviceability policies should include a consistent set of serviceability criteria across all mortgage products; where different criteria are used for different products or brands, APRA expects the ADI to be able to articulate the commercial reasons and implications for risk profile.

A sound serviceability assessment should include consideration of all existing and ongoing debt commitments (secured and unsecured), interest rates, outstanding principal and evidence of delinquency, and should not rely on longer-term access to honeymoon or discounted introductory rates.

Under Attachment C of APS 220, ADIs must apply a serviceability buffer of at least 3.0 per cent over the loan's interest rate (ignoring honeymoon rates), applied to both new and existing debt commitments.

A prudent ADI should use the serviceability buffer in conjunction with an interest rate floor to ensure the buffer is adequate in a low interest rate environment, and should regularly review buffers and floors on at least a quarterly basis and when interest rates change.

APRA expects ADIs to fully apply buffers and floor rates to both new and existing debt commitments, making sufficient enquiries on existing debt including current interest rate, remaining term, outstanding balance, redraw availability and evidence of delinquency.

APRA expects ADIs to use a suitably prudent period for assessing repayment of credit card or other revolving personal debt, for example assessing repayment obligations at three per cent per month on the total committed limit.

For interest-only loans, APRA expects ADIs to assess the borrower's ability to meet future repayments on a principal and interest basis for the specific term over which principal and interest repayments apply, excluding the interest-only period.

A prudent ADI should discount or disregard temporarily high or uncertain income and apply appropriate adjustments to seasonal or variable income sources; prudent practice is to apply discounts of at least 20 per cent on most types of non-salary income.

A prudent ADI should make reasonable inquiries and take reasonable steps to verify a borrower's available income through a combination of factors including employment status confirmation, payslips, employer/accountant advice, tax returns, bank statements and credit history enquiries.

A prudent ADI should make reasonable inquiries and take reasonable steps to verify a self-employed borrower's available income through income and cash flow verification and supporting documentation including third-party verification.

In APRA's view, prudent serviceability policies should incorporate a minimum haircut of at least 20 per cent on expected rental income, with larger haircuts appropriate for properties with higher risk of non-occupancy.

A prudent ADI should account for investment property-related fees and expenses in serviceability assessments and should place no reliance on a borrower's potential ability to access future tax benefits from operating a rental property at a loss.

APRA expects ADIs to use the greater of a borrower's declared living expenses or an appropriately scaled version of the HEM or HPI indices, applying a margin linked to the borrower's income to the relevant index, and updating these indices at least in line with published updates (typically quarterly).

A prudent ADI should include a reasonable estimate of housing costs in serviceability assessments even if a borrower does not currently report personal housing expenses due to living arrangements with friends or relatives.
A prudent ADI should have effective procedures to verify a potential borrower's existing debt commitments and take reasonable steps to identify undeclared debt commitments.

A prudent ADI should retain complete documentation of information supporting a residential mortgage approval, including income and expense documentation and verification steps, for a reasonable number of years after origination.

A prudent ADI's risk limits should reflect the maximum level of allowable overrides and be supported by a robust monitoring framework; APRA expects that where overrides breach risk limits, appropriate action would be taken by senior management.

APRA expects an ADI to have a framework that clearly defines overrides, capturing any loan approved outside serviceability criteria parameters including loans with net income surplus less than $0 or where exceptions to minimum serviceability requirements have been granted.

A sound override framework should detail the approval process, documentary requirements, acceptable reasons for an override and an oversight mechanism to monitor and report overrides; regular override reporting should be provided to senior management and the Board.

APRA expects an ADI to have appropriate processes to determine whether loans approved through overrides to internal policy would be treated as standard or non-standard loans for capital purposes under APS 112.

APRA expects an ADI to consider a borrower's HELP debt obligations alongside other debt commitments when assessing borrowing capacity, as HELP repayments are deducted from gross income and are not available to service a mortgage.

An ADI retains both the risk and responsibility for material outsourced functions including origination and ongoing management of residential mortgage lending; where lending authority is delegated to third parties, robust oversight mechanisms must be implemented and adherence to policies subject to regular audit.

A prudent ADI should have appropriate procedures to verify the accuracy and completeness of information provided by third-party originators; income verification should preferably be performed by the ADI rather than a third party, and third-party approval and verification processes should be periodically monitored and tested.

A prudent ADI should ensure that arrangements with third-party residential mortgage loan originators allow for timely cessation of such arrangements should the ADI no longer be able to place reliance on the third party.

Scorecards or models used in residential mortgage loan evaluation should be subject to regular monitoring and validation; a prudent ADI should develop an effective governance framework and benchmarks to assess scorecard performance, and should seek independent third-party validation where vendor conflict of interest exists.

APRA expects an ADI to recognise in its risk appetite portfolio limits for loan types more vulnerable to serviceability stress and property value decreases, including interest-only, foreign currency, non-standard documentation, reverse mortgage, HELOC and SMSF loans.

APRA expects that an ADI would only approve interest-only loans for owner-occupiers where there is a sound and documented economic basis, and not based on inability to service a loan on a principal and interest basis; interest-only periods should be of limited duration.

In APRA's view, it is not prudent to extend foreign currency-denominated loans against domestic currency income streams or domestic currency collateral; where foreign currency loans are extended to borrowers with foreign currency income, offshore income should be discounted in assessing loan servicing capacity.

For loans with non-standard/alternative documentation, a prudent ADI should seek to address increased risk through appropriate pricing and significantly lower LVRs, and should ensure such loans are warranted by the borrower's situation rather than reluctance to provide documentation.

An ADI undertaking reverse mortgage lending should take appropriate measures to address unique operational, legal and reputational risks, including assessment of actuarial advice, LVR caps related to borrower age, regular revaluation procedures and cautioning borrowers against waiving independent advice.
A prudent ADI should establish checks and portfolio limits for HELOC loans as part of its risk appetite statement, including limits on the non-amortising portion of such loans.

APRA expects that a prudent ADI would identify the additional risks relevant to SMSF lending and implement loan application assessment processes and criteria that adequately reflect these risks; a decision to undertake SMSF lending should be approved at an appropriate governance forum and explicitly incorporated into the ADI's policy framework.
APRA expects that an ADI will document its valuation policy; as collateral risk increases or loan coverage by collateral decreases, the need for specialist valuation also increases.

A prudent ADI contemplating use of alternative valuation methods such as AVMs should subject proposals to thorough analysis and develop a risk management capability including a hierarchy of acceptable methods, analysis of strengths and weaknesses, back-testing, ongoing monitoring and appropriate staff training.

Where an ADI relies on a panel of approved valuation professionals, the panel should be periodically reviewed by senior risk management staff; valuer selection should be conducted by the risk management area rather than sales staff.

Sound risk management practices for security valuation should include valuation reports prepared with professional skill and diligence, valuers selected on the basis of appropriate professional qualifications and comprehensive valuation documentation maintained for the term of the loan.

When valuation management is outsourced, oversight and control should be retained by the ADI's credit risk management; any override of valuation requirements should be limited to senior credit risk management of the ADI.

Good practice requires ADIs to ensure claims against collateral are legally enforceable, including confirming clear title, that property characteristics are as represented, and that collateral is appropriately insured at origination and maintained under contractual mortgage terms.

A prudent ADI should monitor exposures by LVR bands over time; significant increases in high LVR lending should typically trigger senior management review of risk targets and internal controls over high LVR lending, with Board oversight.

Sound credit practice should include recalculating LVRs at the time of any top-up loan, formal loan increases, refinancing or significant draw-down on equity, based on appropriate and contemporary property valuations.

For off-the-plan sales, a prudent ADI should make appropriate reductions in developer prices when determining LVRs or seek independent professional valuations; developer discounts should not be treated as part of the borrower's deposit for LVR calculation purposes.

APRA does not consider the sole use of LMI coverage as a sufficient control to mitigate high LVR risk; where higher LVR lending is permitted, additional risk should be mitigated by stronger serviceability-adjusted loan pricing and, for IRB banks, higher expected loss provisions and capital.

A prudent ADI should assess the guarantor's income and conduct independent checks on creditworthiness, enforceability of potential claims and value of collateral pledged by the guarantor, and should consider appropriate portfolio limits for guarantor lending.

APRA expects that an ADI, as part of its credit risk appetite framework, will define its approach to resolving troubled loans both individually and under conditions where an unusually large number of borrowers are distressed simultaneously.

An ADI should have a full understanding of the risk profile of hardship loans and ensure these risks are appropriately reflected in internal management reporting, provisioning and capital adequacy calculations.

Where a residential mortgage loan is in default, a prudent ADI should usually undertake a full revaluation when assessing the value of the collateral; upon receiving a hardship notice, the ADI should re-assess the borrower's income, living expenses, assets and liabilities.

For prudential reporting purposes, ADIs should not freeze or re-age loans where hardship concessions have been granted; arrears should continue to accrue based on original scheduled payments until the loan is brought back into performing status.

Hardship or collections decisions involving ongoing concessions beyond normally available terms (non-commercial terms) must be reported as restructured items in APRA prudential returns, and appropriate loan-loss collective provisioning should be maintained for loans subject to hardship applications.

A prudent ADI should regularly stress test its residential mortgage lending portfolio under a range of severe but plausible adverse scenarios, with complexity and granularity taking into account the scale and risk characteristics of the portfolio.

Stress testing should be conducted at a sufficiently granular level to capture risk characteristics including product type, LVR, LMI coverage, serviceability, geography, vintage, origination channel and borrower characteristics; scenario and modelling assumptions should be communicated to senior management.

Stress test models should be validated and checked by an independent internal group or appropriately qualified external party; vendor models should be appropriately customised to the ADI's specific risk profile.

Stress testing arrangements should include well-documented policies and procedures governing the stress testing program, with timely communication of results to the Board, senior management and relevant staff, including quantitative information on scenarios, results, capital impact, key assumptions and recommended actions.

LMI is not an alternative to loan origination due diligence; a prudent ADI should, notwithstanding LMI coverage, conduct its own due diligence including independent assessment of borrower repayment capacity, verification of minimum initial equity, reasonable debt service coverage and property value assessment.

An ADI must ensure compliance with all LMI provider requirements to avoid invalidating cover in the event of a claim; good practice is for internal audit and/or risk management functions to periodically test and confirm compliance with LMI provider requirements.
APRA expects an ADI to have its own hindsight review and audit regime and not to rely solely on the audit and compliance regimes of LMI providers.
